Located on an 87-acre site in Shenandoah County, the project is designed to meet the growing demand for compute-intensive workloads from hyperscalers and AI platforms. The site will feature two-story buildings and is part of a broader portfolio that includes over 1,600 acres and more than 2.8GW of planned IT capacity across multiple U.S. states. What sets Project Tallmadge apart is Takanock’s integrated approach to digital and power infrastructure. The company’s patent-pending utility yard design incorporates on-site natural gas generation, enabling the campus to operate independently until grid connections are fully established. This model accelerates time to power and enhances grid flexibility—key advantages in constrained Tier I markets like Northern Virginia. Backed by $500 million in capital from DigitalBridge and ArcLight Capital, Takanock aims to sell shovel-ready sites to data center operators. Construction on Project Tallmadge is expected to begin in late 2027, with completion around 2030. The project reflects Takanock’s strategy of bridging energy and digital infrastructure to deliver scalable, resilient solutions for the next generation of data centers. The measure of Data Center resiliency (availability) is called the “Tier rating system”. These standards are administered by The Uptime Institute (http://uptimeinstitute.org). Tier I Tier I is composed of a single path for power and cooling distribution, without redundant components (99.671% availability). Tier II Tier II is composed of a single path for power and cooling distribution, with redundant components (99.741% availability). Tier III Tier III is composed of multiple active power and cooling distribution paths, but only one path active, has redundant components, and is Concurrently Maintainable (99.982% availability). Tier IV Tier IV: composed of multiple active power and cooling distribution paths, has redundant components, and is fault tolerant (99.995% availability). The Uptime Institute exclusively contracts ComputerSite Engineering to conduct thorough reviews of a data center for the certification process. Address: CloudHQ 9651 Hornbaker Road 20109 Manassas Virginia, USA
Description: Data CentersUSAVirginiaManassas Cloud Capital - 9651 Hornbaker CloudHQ 9651 Hornbaker Road 20109 Manassas Virginia, USA Visit Website Suites Profile text written prior to data center being acquired by Cloud Capital (CloudHQ): Power Loft LLC was formed in 2006 as a joint venture between Oak Hill Realty LLC, the real estate investment arm of the Robert M. Bass Family (now DBA Iron Point Partners LLC), and Coakley Investors LLC. Iron Point Partners raised $460 million in private equity in 2008 to support the Power Loft in the development of mission critical facilities and will remain involved as the lead capital partner throughout any proposed development for the Federal Government. Power Loft facilities have four primary distinctions over datacenters built to date: 1.Scaling: Power Loft data centers differ in their ability to physically scale in modular increments of 50,000 raised square feet, and in power increments ranging from 100 to over 300 watts per raised square foot, but with no attrition to the original raised floor area. 2.High Density: Our proprietary two story design segregates all IT equipment one story above the mechanical and electric plant, with all CRAHs being consolidated off of the raised floor area into larger, more efficient air handling units below. This results in space, cooling and power for 50% more racks, or three times the computing power over comparably platforms. 3.High Security: Power Loft data centers comply with Defense Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA) design guidelines --bermed, fenced, gated with rollup vehicle barriers and guarded 24/7. Our two story design also promotes a hard separation between the raised floor area above and the MEP activities below. 4.Green: All of our buildings are LEED Certified. Our two story design utilizes 50% less land than comparable data centers while supporting 50% more racks and 50% more power per square foot of raised floor area. Our green roof and green screen minimize impact on the local watershed and insulate the building from fluctuations in outside temperatures. And our unique AC/DC power backbone will accommodate the latest advances in the use of DC powered blade servers. Combined, Power Loft data center accommodate three times the computing power with the ability to use 36% less power. In addition, the ability to provide critical power in measured increments allow users to step up their rental commitments slightly in advance of demand. Strategically proposed for a 103-acre site, VA12 will be part of a visionary 336MW hyperscale campus designed to support the future demands of AI, cloud computing, and enterprise workloads. Once developed, VA12 will offer 96MW of critical IT load across 336,000 square feet of IT space, housed within one of four planned two-story buildings reaching up to 75 feet in height. Security and operational excellence are central to the VA12 concept, featuring multi-layered physical protection, real-time surveillance, and high-availability standards. The proposed location is adjacent to major fiber routes and a planned Dominion Energy substation, ensuring robust connectivity and low-latency access to global networks. In line with NTT’s commitment to sustainability and innovation, VA12 is designed to meet LEED standards, emphasizing its position as the world’s third-largest data center provider. This future-ready facility will be tailored for hyperscale growth and digital transformation once developed. Spanning 1,143 acres, the campus will feature 11 two-story data center buildings and one office building, designed using Amazon's standardized layouts. The project aims to deliver a minimum of 770MW of data center capacity by 2027, addressing the growing demand for computing and data storage infrastructure. The campus will include access roads, parking lots, dock areas, stormwater management facilities, and three substation pads to connect the campus to the electrical grid. This initiative is part of Amazon's broader commitment to invest $35 billion in Virginia's data center infrastructure by 2040, with the goal of establishing a data center in every county between Northern Virginia and Richmond. The Mattermeade project underscores Amazon's dedication to expanding its digital infrastructure while supporting local economic growth and technological advancement. Description: Data CentersUSAVirginiaManassas EvoSwitch Manassas (WDC1) EvoSwitch 9651 Hornbaker Road 20109 Manassas Virginia, USA Visit Website Suites Cages Footprints Private Cabinets Partial Cabinets Remote Hands EvoSwitch WDC1 EvoSwitch´s new WDC1 data center is located in Manassas, VA near Washington, D.C., inside northern Virginia's technology corridor. The NoVa region acts as one of the world's main interconnection hubs for the Internet. The area's data centers attract a high density of communications, cloud and digital media companies for colocation and interconnection services companies that represent key segments for EvoSwitch. The WDC1 data center in Virginia is located on a secure, 20-acre site within the 1,500 acre Innovation Park. WDC1 is LEED Gold certified and DOD- and DCID compliant and measures approximately 230,000 square feet (gross space) and will be phased into service. This innovative data center features a unique two level design that places the 100,000 square feet of raised floor space on the second level with the accompanying equipment directly beneath it on the first level, creating significant efficiencies as well as the ability to increase critical power to keep pace with market demand for more densely powered space. Full critical load is expected to be up to 30 megawatts. New!
